NANCY RYTI'S DEMURRER IS ORDERED OFF CALENDAR FOR NONCOMPLIANCE WITH CCP 430.41, WHICH REQUIRES THAT THE PARTIES CONDUCT A "MEET AND CONFER IN PERSON OR BY TELEPHONE" BEFORE A DEMURRER IS FILED. A DECLARATION MUST THEN BE FILED WHICH SPECIFIES WHETHER THE MEET AND CONFER WAS IN PERSON OR BY TELEPHONE. THE PARTIES ARE ORDERED TO MEET AND CONFER IMMEDIATELY AND THE RESPONSE, IF ANY, IS NOW DUE BY APRIL 17, 2023. JUDGE: RICHARD B. ULMER JR., CLERK: M. GOODMAN, NOT REPORTED. (302/RBU)

CASE MANAGEMENT CONFERENCE
Off Calendar Jul-19-2024 Set For Trial On Jan-27-25 At 09:30 A.M. In Dept 206

Notice Of Motion And Motion To Strike Or Tax Defendant Solstice Constructions Costs, Memorandum Of Points And Authorities And Declaration Of Nany Ryti In Support; [Proposed] Order
Off Calendar Jul-19-2024 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:

HEARING RE: PLAINTIFF NANCY RYTI's Motion To Strike Or Tax Defendant Solstice Constructions Costs.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling.

Plaintiff's motion to tax is granted and denied in part.

This costs memorandum is timely. The order of dismissal in defendant's favor filed on May 24, 2024, qualifies as a judgment per CCP 680.230 ["'Judgment' means a judgment, order, or decree entered in a court of this state."]. "An order of dismissal is an appealable 'judgment' if it is (i) in writing, (ii) signed by the court, and (iii) filed in the action." (Roe v. Doe (2023) 98 Cal.App.5th 965, 972; CCP 581d ["All dismissals ordered by the court shall be in the form of a written order signed by the court and filed in the action and those orders when so filed shall constitute judgments and be effective for all purposes, and the clerk shall note those judgments in the register of actions in the case."].)

Defendant is the prevailing party and entitled to costs as a matter of right. (CCP 1032(a)(4) [defining prevailing party as defendant who obtains a dismissal].) Plaintiff's citation to Kaufman v. Pacific Indem. Co. (1936) 5 Cal.2d 761, 769 is off point. There, the court merely notes that costs are not recoverable where a judgment expressly provides that they are not.

"If the items appearing in a cost bill appear to be proper charges, the burden is on the party seeking to tax costs to show that they were not reasonable or necessary. On the other hand, if the items are properly objected to, they are put in issue and the burden of proof is on the party claiming them as costs." (Ladas v. California State Auto. Assn. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 774.

The costs memorandum claims $1,414.95 in costs.

The court strikes item 1(b) [motion to dismiss $475.35]. That was an improper combined motion that included codefendants, who have yet to prevail in this action.

The court strikes part of item 1(c). Defendant Solstice is entitled to recover the reasonable costs of the motion to dismiss: the e-filing fee ($11.50), the motion fee ($60), processing fee ($2.15), the courtesy copy delivery fee ($80), and processing fee ($2.40). ($156.05). The court can allow for the recovery of messenger fees in its discretion and concludes that the courtesy copy/delivery fee is such a cost. (Ladas v. California State Auto. Assn. (1993) 19 Cal.App.4th 761, 776 [allowing recovery for deliveries/messengering].) Defendant also substantiates the proof of service and delivery fee of $94.25 (item 1(d)). (Palenchar Decl., Ex. D.)

The court therefore strikes $794.65 and defendant is entitled to a total of $620.30.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East; Clerk: S. Kane; Not Reported. (302/RCE)

MINI MINUTES:
Hearing re: Defendant Solstice Construction's Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Solstice Construction's motion to dismiss is granted.

Plaintiff filed this action on February 28, 2018, and named movant Solstice Construction in the original complaint. Code of Civil Procedure ("CCP") section 583.310 states that "[a]n action shall be brought to trial within five years after the action is commenced against the defendant." CCP 583.340(c) provides that the five-year period can be tolled when it is "impracticable" to bring the action to trial. Plaintiff's opposition cites several reasons for the delay. Plaintiff generally contends that Solstice Construction evaded service and notes that her counsel passed away. Even if true, plaintiff does not argue that tolling applies and does not present evidence showing the precise length of tolling. Plaintiff's evidence (a declaration filed on February 6, 2023) is insufficient and plaintiff filed this action over six years ago. Even if the court adds the six-month extension of time to bring an action to trial per COVID 19 Emergency Rule 10(a), the mandatory five-year deadline has expired. The court has no discretion and the action is dismissed.

Order signed.

Judge: Rochelle C. East. Clerk: Jacqueline Alameda. Not Reported. Bailiff: Park. (301/RCE)

Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice
Off Calendar May-24-2024 Denied
MINI MINUTES:
Hearing re: Defendant Lawrence Sweeny's Motion To Dismiss With Prejudice.

No appearances made in this matter. The Court adopts its tentative ruling as follows:

Defendant Sweeny's motion to dismiss is denied without prejudice. Code of Civil Procedure section 583.420(a)(1) gives the court the discretion to dismiss an action where the plaintiff fails to serve the defendant within two years of filing the action against the defendant. In this case, plaintiff's counsel died and while in pro per, plaintiff encountered difficulties in naming and serving movant. The COVID 19 pandemic also caused prosecutorial delay. Given these facts, the motion is denied without prejudice but may be renewed if new facts arise showing a lack of diligence. Plaintiff to obtain a trial date from department 206.
